item.title: This hit hard.
item.content:encoded: Easily one of the best Series VI articles. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came into this knowing about The Caretaker and expected this to totally destroy me. This did not disappoint! This is beautiful and I hate it, but in a good way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sobering.

dementia runs in the family.
this'll be me one day.

+1

item.title: oh ok
item.content:encoded:
I read this while listening to the music in the title link. Stage 6 is creepy as heck. And so is this article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been a long time since something has stirred me emotionally like this. I don't really know what else to say.

Fantastically done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm here after the contest ended, but this is the reason I finally broke down and applied for the SCP Foundation. I have no words. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely heart wrenching work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very powerful comment for me. I'm really sorry to hear about your grandmother, I fear something like that happening to anyone I'm close to, and cannot fully imagine secondhand effects of having someone you know suffer from dementia.

I similarly fear that happening to me when I get older. My answer to the ultimatum of either facing whatever happens after death or letting my mind slowly decay would be the same as yours. I can only imagine it to be torture.

This article being able to elicit these same feelings — these same fears — into others who've had much worse experiences than me with dementia makes me feel that I actually did something right during the creation of it.

I'm truly grateful for this comment. What you said really means a lot to me. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a contest marshal, I was intending to comment on the entries after the contest was over, to avoid any possibility of undue influence. I could not bear to do that on this article.

My grandmother, whom I love very dearly, is suffering from dementia. Seeing her lose more of herself every time we see each other has been and continues to be one of the most painful, terrifying things I've ever experienced.

I was considered a gifted child, and between my mother's unhealthy emotional behavior towards me and my own anxiety and sensitivity, I came to believe (and probably still DO believe unconsciously) that my mind is the only part of me worth anything. The thought of feeling the slow decay of my own mind, the reduction from a full complex human being into a confused shell of myself, terrifies me to my core. For context: I am regularly overcome with an intense fear of the nothingness after death, but I would sooner take oblivion than live through this.

All of this to say that this article pinned me down, cracked my ribcage open, and tore into my very soul. You captured it - what I've often imagined must go inside the minds of those afflicted with such a disease. I even had inklings from the very start, reading the "Local Guide's" dialogue, thinking it felt.. simplistic, repetitive. The confusion, the shattering of the internal timeline, the frustration, the fear - all of it poignant and raw and real.

The easiest +1 I've given in the entire contest, and also by far the most painful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're talking about the dialogue tags in the last log, then yes that is absolutely intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was the abnormal capitalization intentional?
Take care. It's a desert out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
also, minorly spoilery but…

+ show block
– hide block

I love that the administrator is a woman, and a sapphic one at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: time to fly up away into the sun like a fucking piece of garbage. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as has been said already many times… this didn't click for me till the "edit". then I went "ohhhhhhhh" and after the end, I sort of just… stared openmouthed at my phone for awhile.

my grandmother died of cancer, not dementia. but by the time her body finally gave in, her mind was already gone. watching her be stolen away from me, piece by piece — I was 12-14 and responsible for her care — is one of the worst things I've ever had to see. this took me back there, and I'm misty, but it's not a bad thing. an unreserved +1. thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good read with a serious topic that comes with a lot to think about. The anomaly in actuality being something that is simply described by the plane debris is brilliant. Although, to my knowledge, nobody in my family has had dementia, this still hits close to home in a few ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this gave me the exact same chills i had when i read 4012, and the exact same mistiness on that last line when as when i read the end of that SCP. truly beautiful, well executed, without going overboard on the format screw. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Minstrata screams. She remembers screaming, needing the prolonged comfort of anything she can fully, truly remember. She remembers what screaming means. She keeps screaming because of that.)

I have a lot of memory issues, and it feels like every week, like clockwork, I have an existential crisis about it. It's something I've gotten used to; it's not easy to deal with, but it's become routine, so I barely even notice it. This skip in general — but particularly that line — spoke to me. There's a certain terror in not knowing what's happened in your past; you just keep retracing the lines of the three and a half events you actually remember, trying to figure out the shape of the rest of them, and that shape is never good. Even small things (like not remembering the color of the shirt you're wearing or not remembering getting your keys despite having them on you) can send you into a spiral… or at least it does for me.

Anyway, this gets a +1 from me. I think you captured the essence of that terror really well, and in a way that's evocative and beautifully written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got a little 3999 there, didn't ya? I really dig this. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That minor error has been corrected.

I'm not good at things, you see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you Conwell, writing the imagery for the last log (and writing the last log in general) was definitely my favorite part of making this. i think you forgot to vote though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, that last log has got some good imagery to it. As with others, I didn't quite get what was going on until the final log, notably at the mention of Galantamine. While this isn't the first stab of dementia we've seen on the site, it certainly is one of the more unique ones.

+1. Quality article, Laneous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, the way you wrote this is just so powerful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote from me. I did not comprehend the metaphorical nature of the plane until the last log. I especially like the detail of it crashing over and over again every time someone uses it. That feeling of the experience suddenly cutting off is very well reflected in that revision.

Also, someday I will listen to the album you linked in it's entirety. Maybe if I want to try an extended writing session or if I'm working on a game project. I love the vibe of what I've listened to so far.

I was about to downvote when i saw the "revision pending" line. "Sheesh, yet another magical explanation on demand. What a cheap way to get around narrative restrictions in SCPs." I am so very glad i made it to the end.

I still don't know how i shall feel about this. I have the feeling i am just one small detail from understanding it… which means you executed that whole "mystery" thing pretty well. You left me intrigued.

It also knows when to stop, instead of dragging on eternally like certain other contest entries.

Well… i don't know what else to say. I hope this gets the slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
even when you see the entire bedroom, there's still the corner behind you, right behind you. that's where the read bad things are.

also nice meme

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Caretaker was a massive inspiration for this article, I couldn't have been able to write this if I hadn't listed to Everywhere at the End of Time about 3-4 times over. I'm really happy that I captured some of the energy that album had in here.

I appreciate the comment and the read. thanks modulum!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm glad this works for you, and other people, (and i hope this does good too).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I don't get the references to the Administrator though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this made me crawl into bed in that portion up against the corner where two walls meet so I can see my entire bedroom at once. And it's been a while since I've read an article that gave me that kind of reaction.

Unabashed +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The prose here is heartwrenching, and extremely well done. With the context of The Caretaker as a well of inspiration for the piece, the ballroom imagery in the final log becomes all the more meaningful. I really love the poetic nature of the recursive plane - how it both works thematically and just feels melancholy in atmosphere - as well as the final lines of the log, which mix a bittersweet feeling of peace with a crushing sense of finality. The way that you've blended these emotions together in an ultimately very simple and thus more open way is absolutely commendable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, just wow, I would have upvoted for the original anomaly, the recusrive plane exploring a broken dimension really works for me, but that last log is a home fucking run my man. The descent into dementia, and the original anomaly itself being a metaphor (the plane for someone broken by dementia and the recursive dimensions for the process itself), absolute gold. +1 without hesitation, and I sincerely hope you do well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it in the end. Thank you!

I think that "peaks" in the final log might need to be "peeks".

Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was not sold until the last log, but there is some very effective writing there, as well as a solid exploration of the core idea, and that was enough for me to upvote. Too many lovely bits to credit them all, but "(Television Static.)", "(Hospitals.)" and "an elegant shade of color" stood out.

I think that "peaks" in the final log might need to be "peeks".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May the ballroom remain eternal.

— The Caretaker

Memory loss and dementia are big fears of mine. The thought of just forgetting everything that made you who you are is just… viscerally brutal to me. The slow degradation of the mind in general unnerves me to no end.

Unless I missed some glaring grammatical errors, everything in the last log is exactly how it should be.

Critique People:

  • Almarduk — SCP-4875
  • plaidypus — SCP-5335
  • Popsioak — SCP-5111
  • Deadly Bread — SCP-3462
  • psychicprogrammer
  • Rounzie — Memetics and Infohazards Division Orientation, While this critter didn't read this draft, they read the draft for my original idea and inadvertently pushed me away from it for the contest at least, causing me to eventually pursue this.

Image Source:

  • plane.jpg — CC BY 2.0

Slot Preference: 1: 5999, 2: 5555, 3: 5666, 4. 5005, 5: 5678

EDIT: Oh yeah, we in the scary slot now.
